ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for teriyaki sauce; mapped to soy-sauce-based condiment category A0CKW as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for teriyaki sauce as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of teriyaki sauce is approximately 1 tablespoon (15 g), used as a glaze, marinade, or dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Teriyaki sauce, ready-to-serve — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo database entry for teriyaki sauce (cr_id F10050004), confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients sourced from this record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for teriyaki sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on the analogous food 'soy sauce-based condiment with sugar' from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dominant carbohydrate source is sucrose and glucose from sugar and mirin; GI of sucrose is approximately 65 and mirin-derived glucose raises the blended estimate. A conservative blended estimate of 50 is applied. Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (50 × 27.5 × 0.15) / 100 = 2.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 3591 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Teriyaki sauce is contraindicated for individuals with hypertension; even a single 15 g tablespoon delivers approximately 539 mg sodium — nearly 90% of the 600 mg per-meal sodium target — and must be strictly limited or replaced with a reduced-sodium alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content at 17.7 g per 100 g with negligible dietary fibre (0.6 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high free-sugar load from mirin and added sugar (17.7 g/100 g) with minimal fibre (0.6 g/100 g) causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict teriyaki sauce to very small quantities and account for its carbohydrate contribution within their daily glucose management plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium (3591 mg/100 g) and elevated sugar (17.7 g/100 g) contributing to blood pressure and metabolic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of extreme sodium and high sugar in teriyaki sauce poses a dual cardiovascular risk by elevating blood pressure and promoting dyslipidaemia; individuals with cardiovascular disease should use this condiment only in trace amounts or substitute with a low-sodium, reduced-sugar variant.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (3591 mg/100 g) worsening tissue oedema and high sugar (17.7 g/100 g) promoting pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise teriyaki sauce use due to its pro-inflammatory sodium and sugar load; a homemade version using reduced-sodium soy sauce, minimal sugar, and added ginger or garlic provides a less harmful alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teriyaki sauce (照り焼きソース, teriyaki sōsu) is a classic Japanese condiment and cooking glaze made by combining soy sauce, mirin (sweet rice wine), sake, and sugar, then reducing the mixture until it thickens into a glossy, sweet-savoury coating. The sauce has a deep umami flavour balanced by pronounced sweetness, a slightly syrupy texture, and a characteristic caramelised aroma from the reduction process. It is used as a marinade, basting glaze, or dipping sauce for grilled meats, fish, tofu, and vegetables. Per 100 g, teriyaki sauce provides 123 kcal, 3.1 g protein, 0.1 g fat, and 27.5 g carbohydrate of which 17.7 g is sugar. Sodium is extremely high at 3591 mg per 100 g, making it one of the highest-sodium condiments in common use. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0.6 g per 100 g. Clinically, teriyaki sauce is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its extreme sodium and high free-sugar content. Even a standard 15 g serving delivers approximately 539 mg sodium and 2.7 g sugar. Reduced-sodium and reduced-sugar commercial variants are available and are strongly preferred for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in teriyaki sauce?
Is teriyaki sauce high in sugar?
What is teriyaki sauce made of?
How many calories in teriyaki sauce?
Is teriyaki sauce suitable for hypertension?
What is a low-sodium alternative to teriyaki sauce?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10050004) with confidence 0.95. The food is a well-defined commercial condiment with stable composition. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021) as no direct GI measurement exists for teriyaki sauce.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0: teriyaki sauce is a plant-based, fat-negligible condiment (total fat 0.1 g/100 g) with confirmed negligible saturated fat per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source; teriyaki sauce contains no animal-derived fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for teriyaki sauce are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F10050004 and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact teriyaki sauce match as of 2026-04-16. GI is an analogous estimate of 50 (low) based on soy-sauce-based condiment with sugar from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for teriyaki sauce exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Halal status is uncertain due to mirin and sake (alcohol-containing ingredients); marked is_halal: false. Gluten-free status is false due to soy sauce containing wheat.
